questions.question: Explain the two phases of Comparison Counting Sort with an example array [4, 2, 4, 1].
keyPoints: Phase 1: Count occurrences of each element - create count array
Phase 2: Compute cumulative counts to determine positions
Phase 3: Place elements in output array starting from right to maintain stability
Show final sorted output: [1, 2, 4, 4]

questions.question: Why is Counting Sort classified as a non-comparison based sorting algorithm?
keyPoints: Does not use element-to-element comparisons
Uses element values as indices into count array
Bypasses the Ω(n log n) comparison sort lower bound
Achieves linear time O(n + k) when range is small

questions.question: What is the time and space complexity of Counting Sort? Explain when it is efficient.
keyPoints: Time: O(n + k), Space: O(n + k)
Efficient when k = O(n), i.e., range is proportional to number of elements
When k >> n, becomes inefficient due to large count array
Example: sorting 1000 integers from 0-1000 is efficient, from 0-1M is not

questions.question: Explain why Counting Sort is considered a stable sorting algorithm.
keyPoints: Stability means equal elements maintain relative order
Counting Sort processes from right to left in input
When placing elements, uses cumulative count as position
This ensures earlier occurrence of equal value appears earlier in output

questions.question: How would you modify Counting Sort to handle negative integers in the input array?
keyPoints: Find minimum value (min) in the input array
Use offset: index = element - min for count array
Count array size becomes (max - min + 1)
During placement, add back min to get actual value

questions.question: Compare Counting Sort with Quick Sort in terms of time complexity and practical usage.
keyPoints: Quick Sort: O(n log n) average, O(n²) worst - comparison based
Counting Sort: O(n + k) linear when k=O(n), but uses more space
Counting Sort practical for integer sorting with bounded range
Quick Sort is general-purpose, comparison-based, in-place

questions.question: What are the limitations of Comparison Counting Sort?
keyPoints: Requires knowledge of range (max value) beforehand
Not suitable when range k >> number of elements n
Not in-place - requires O(n + k) extra space
Only works with discrete values (integers, characters), not general keys

questions.question: Trace through the complete execution of Comparison Counting Sort on the array A = [2, 5, 3, 2, 4, 1, 3]. Show all steps including count array creation, cumulative counting, and final sorted output.
keyPoints: Find min=1, max=5, k=5
Create initial count array: [1,1,2,2,1] for values 1-5
Cumulative count: [1,2,4,6,7]
Process from right: place 3→pos4, 1→pos1, 4→pos6, 2→pos2, 3→pos5, 5→pos7, 2→pos3
Sorted output: [1,2,2,3,3,4,5]
Emphasize right-to-left traversal for stability

questions.question: Derive and explain the time complexity O(n+k) of Comparison Counting Sort. Why does it achieve linear time while comparison sorts cannot?
keyPoints: Counting phase: O(n) - traverse input once
Cumulative counting: O(k) - traverse count array once
Placement phase: O(n) - traverse output once
Total: O(n + k) = O(n) when k=O(n)
Comparison sorts use element comparisons with Ω(n log n) lower bound
Non-comparison sorts use value as index, bypassing comparison bottleneck
Mathematical proof: can't sort without comparisons in comparison model

questions.question: Design an algorithm to sort strings using Counting Sort as a subroutine. Explain with an example and discuss the time complexity.
keyPoints: Use LSD (Least Significant Digit) Radix Sort approach
Apply Counting Sort from last character to first
Stable Counting Sort ensures correct ordering for multi-character strings
Example: sort ["cat", "bat", "at", "rat"] by last character first
Time complexity: O(w × (n + k)) where w = max string length, k = alphabet size
Space complexity: O(n + k) for each pass

questions.question: Compare and contrast Comparison Counting Sort with Merge Sort. Analyze their performance characteristics, space requirements, and practical use cases.
keyPoints: Time: Counting Sort O(n+k) vs Merge Sort O(n log n)
Space: Counting Sort O(n+k) vs Merge Sort O(n)
Comparison: Counting is non-comparison, Merge is comparison-based
Stability: Both are stable
Use cases: Counting for bounded integers, Merge for general data
Counting requires known range, Merge works on any comparable data
Counting in practice: when k=O(n), it's faster; Merge is versatile
